name: Marzanna
description: Marzanna, also known as Morana, Morena, or Mara, is a complex and multifaceted figure in Slavic mythology, primarily associated with winter, death, and rebirth. She is often depicted as a goddess embodying the harshness of winter, bringing frost, snow, and the dormant period of the agricultural cycle. Marzanna is not simply a goddess of death; she represents the cyclical nature of life, death, and renewal, a crucial element in the Slavic understanding of the cosmos. Her association with winter's demise makes her a symbol of the end of the old year and the anticipation of spring's return. In many Slavic cultures, the ritualistic drowning or burning of an effigy of Marzanna marks the end of winter and the welcoming of spring, symbolizing the defeat of death and the promise of new life. This ritual underscores the belief in the continuous cycle of nature and the hope for a bountiful harvest.
subtitle: Goddess of Winter and Death

keyMyths.title: The Drowning of Marzanna
keyMyths.description: The most well-known myth associated with Marzanna is the ritualistic drowning or burning of her effigy. This act symbolizes the end of winter and the defeat of death. The effigy, often made of straw and dressed in women's clothing, is carried through the village and then either thrown into a river or burned, signifying the banishment of winter and the welcoming of spring. This practice ensures a fertile and prosperous growing season by symbolically killing off the barrenness of winter.

keyMyths.title: Marzanna and Jarilo
keyMyths.description: In some interpretations, Marzanna is seen as the rival or even the antagonist of Jarilo, the god of spring, fertility, and youthful vigor. Their relationship represents the cyclical battle between winter and summer, death and life. Jarilo's triumph over Marzanna signifies the victory of spring and the renewal of nature. This dynamic highlights the interconnectedness of opposing forces in the Slavic worldview and the importance of balance in the natural world.
keyMyths.source: Иванов, Вяч. Вс., Топоров, В. Н. *Исследования в области славянских древностей*.

keyMyths.title: Marzanna as a Force of Nature
keyMyths.description: Beyond her association with death, Marzanna represents the raw, untamed power of nature, particularly in its harsh winter form. She embodies the destructive forces of ice, snow, and the biting cold, which can threaten crops and human survival. This aspect of her character underscores the respect and fear that the ancient Slavs held for the natural world and its capacity for both creation and destruction. She is not necessarily evil, but a necessary part of the cycle.
keyMyths.source: Strzelczyk, Jerzy. *Mity, podania i wierzenia dawnych Słowian*.

sources.author: Gieysztor, Aleksander

sources.description: An influential work exploring the reconstruction of Proto-Slavic mythology, including the interpretations of Marzanna's role in the broader pantheon.
